APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning problem (multi-sense word embeddings) and proposes a reinforcement-learning based framework (sense-selection as an MDP with Q-learning plus a skip-gram style sense representation module), which is of clear interest to top NLP/ML venues. It uses community-standard datasets (large Wikipedia dump, SCWS), compares against strong baselines (word2vec, Neelakantan et al., Bartunov et al.), and reports standard evaluation metrics (Spearman ρ, AvgSimC/MaxSimC), meeting the methodological and evaluation expectations. The focus on scalability (linear-time sense selection), non-parametric sense induction, and explicit discussion of limitations and hyperparameters further indicate correct positioning for the target research community. | B(5): The paper addresses multi-sense word representations — a core NLP/ML topic — and introduces an RL-based sense selection framework that is directly relevant to the community. It evaluates on standard data (Wikipedia training corpus, SCWS) using established metrics (Spearman, AvgSimC/MaxSimC) and compares to appropriate recent baselines, matching expected evaluation standards. The formulation (MDP/Q-learning sense selection plus skip-gram style representation learning) and discussed limitations (exploration, non-parametric sense counts, Q capped at 3 in experiments) are all positioned squarely for top-tier NLP/ML audiences.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/representation-learning problem (multi-sense word embeddings) and proposes a reinforcement-learning based framework (sense-selection as an MDP with Q-learning plus a skip-gram style sense representation module), which is of clear interest to top NLP/ML venues. It uses community-standard datasets (large Wikipedia dump, SCWS), compares against strong baselines (word2vec, Neelakantan et al., Bartunov et al.), and reports standard evaluation metrics (Spearman ρ, AvgSimC/MaxSimC), meeting the methodological and evaluation expectations. The focus on scalability (linear-time sense selection), non-parametric sense induction, and explicit discussion of limitations and hyperparameters further indicate correct positioning for the target research community.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses multi-sense word representations — a core NLP/ML topic — and introduces an RL-based sense selection framework that is directly relevant to the community. It evaluates on standard data (Wikipedia training corpus, SCWS) using established metrics (Spearman, AvgSimC/MaxSimC) and compares to appropriate recent baselines, matching expected evaluation standards. The formulation (MDP/Q-learning sense selection plus skip-gram style representation learning) and discussed limitations (exploration, non-parametric sense counts, Q capped at 3 in experiments) are all positioned squarely for top-tier NLP/ML audiences.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets an important problem—learning multi-sense word embeddings—and introduces a novel DRL-Sense framework that frames sense selection as an MDP with Q-learning, enabling linear-time selection and non-parametric sense number determination. Empirical claims of competitive/SOTA performance on contextual similarity and matching word2vec using 1/100 of training data suggest the approach could change how researchers frame sense induction and representation learning and is likely to be built upon. Reported practical limitations (e.g., exploration issues, joint policy-gradient drawbacks, and experiments capping Q at 3 senses) temper transformative potential but do not negate strong impact within the area and adjacent representation-learning work. | B(4): The paper targets an important problem—learning multi-sense word embeddings—and introduces DRL-Sense, an RL-based sense selection paired with a sense representation module. Its contributions (linear-time sense selection, non-parametric sense count, joint reward-passing Q-learning, and reported competitive/SOTA results on SCWS while matching word2vec using only 1/100 of training data) are likely to influence how researchers model sense selection and efficiency trade-offs. These properties make the approach likely to be adopted and extended. However, practical limitations reported (e.g., using an upper bound Q=3 in experiments and exploration/joint-optimization issues) temper its claim to be fully transformative.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions are clearly stated early (DRL-Sense with sense selection and representation modules and a reward-passing Q-learning formulation). The methodology and limitations are described precisely (MDP formulation, Q-learning, joint-training caveats, exploration issues), and experiments report datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameters. Notation appears consistent and well-defined given the clear use of MDP/Q-value language, and writing quality is noted as good with thorough related-work coverage; minor presentation issues only (e.g., figures/tables not mentioned in the summary) prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from problem motivation to the two-module DRL-Sense method and experiments (methodology description includes the MDP/Q-learning selection module and skip-gram style representation module). The main contributions are stated early and limitations are explicitly discussed (joint policy-gradient drawback, exploration issues, and the Q<=3 experimental constraint), and experiments and baselines are described. Notation and writing are reported as good, though the summary does not comment on the effectiveness of figures/tables or code availability, suggesting only minor presentation gaps remain.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions are clearly stated early (DRL-Sense with sense selection and representation modules and a reward-passing Q-learning formulation). The methodology and limitations are described precisely (MDP formulation, Q-learning, joint-training caveats, exploration issues), and experiments report datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameters. Notation appears consistent and well-defined given the clear use of MDP/Q-value language, and writing quality is noted as good with thorough related-work coverage; minor presentation issues only (e.g., figures/tables not mentioned in the summary) prevent a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from problem motivation to the two-module DRL-Sense method and experiments (methodology description includes the MDP/Q-learning selection module and skip-gram style representation module). The main contributions are stated early and limitations are explicitly discussed (joint policy-gradient drawback, exploration issues, and the Q<=3 experimental constraint), and experiments and baselines are described. Notation and writing are reported as good, though the summary does not comment on the effectiveness of figures/tables or code availability, suggesting only minor presentation gaps remain.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a notably new perspective by formulating sense selection as an MDP and using a reward-passing Q-learning formulation to jointly train sense selection and representations—an approach that clearly differs from prior multi-sense embedding work (e.g., Neelakantan et al., Bartunov et al.). The use of standard skip-gram negative sampling and context embeddings means parts of the pipeline build on well-established techniques, and experiments still impose an upper bound of Q=3 senses per word, so the contribution is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated extension rather than a radical departure. | B(4): The paper introduces a distinctly new formulation by casting sense selection as an MDP and applying a reward‑passing Q‑learning approach to jointly train selection and representation, which is different from prior multi‑sense embedding methods. It also claims linear‑time sense selection by exploiting word embeddings and supports non‑parametric learning of sense counts, clearly differentiating its techniques from prior work (e.g., Neelakantan et al., Bartunov et al.) listed as baselines. While these are non‑trivial and informative innovations, they build on an established line of multi‑sense embedding research rather than overturning it, so the contribution is notably novel but not radically transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a notably new perspective by formulating sense selection as an MDP and using a reward-passing Q-learning formulation to jointly train sense selection and representations—an approach that clearly differs from prior multi-sense embedding work (e.g., Neelakantan et al., Bartunov et al.). The use of standard skip-gram negative sampling and context embeddings means parts of the pipeline build on well-established techniques, and experiments still impose an upper bound of Q=3 senses per word, so the contribution is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated extension rather than a radical departure.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a distinctly new formulation by casting sense selection as an MDP and applying a reward‑passing Q‑learning approach to jointly train selection and representation, which is different from prior multi‑sense embedding methods. It also claims linear‑time sense selection by exploiting word embeddings and supports non‑parametric learning of sense counts, clearly differentiating its techniques from prior work (e.g., Neelakantan et al., Bartunov et al.) listed as baselines. While these are non‑trivial and informative innovations, they build on an established line of multi‑sense embedding research rather than overturning it, so the contribution is notably novel but not radically transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments are broadly comprehensive: they train on a large Wikipedia corpus (~1B tokens), evaluate on SCWS with standard metrics (AvgSimC/MaxSimC, Spearman ρ), and compare to a wide range of prior multi-sense and single-sense baselines including word2vec. The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses concrete limitations (e.g., drawbacks of a joint policy-gradient formulation, exploration issues with greedy selection), but does not explicitly report ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., effect of the Q upper bound). The non-parametric claim is only partially validated in practice since experiments set an upper-bound Q=3 for fair comparison, and code availability is not mentioned, so full reproducibility and validation of the non-parametric behavior are not fully demonstrated. | B(4): The experiments compare DRL-Sense to a broad set of strong baselines on contextual word similarity (SCWS) and report efficiency/data-efficiency claims using a large Wikipedia corpus, which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is only partially addressed (discussion of the joint policy-gradient drawback and the need for exploration like dropout) but explicit ablation studies or broader downstream evaluations are not reported; experiments also constrain the number of senses per word to an upper bound of 3 for comparisons. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several failure modes/limitations are discussed; reproducibility is reasonably supported by reported hyperparameters and dataset choice, though code availability is not mentioned.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are broadly comprehensive: they train on a large Wikipedia corpus (~1B tokens), evaluate on SCWS with standard metrics (AvgSimC/MaxSimC, Spearman ρ), and compare to a wide range of prior multi-sense and single-sense baselines including word2vec. The paper reports hyperparameters and discusses concrete limitations (e.g., drawbacks of a joint policy-gradient formulation, exploration issues with greedy selection), but does not explicitly report ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., effect of the Q upper bound). The non-parametric claim is only partially validated in practice since experiments set an upper-bound Q=3 for fair comparison, and code availability is not mentioned, so full reproducibility and validation of the non-parametric behavior are not fully demonstrated.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare DRL-Sense to a broad set of strong baselines on contextual word similarity (SCWS) and report efficiency/data-efficiency claims using a large Wikipedia corpus, which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is only partially addressed (discussion of the joint policy-gradient drawback and the need for exploration like dropout) but explicit ablation studies or broader downstream evaluations are not reported; experiments also constrain the number of senses per word to an upper bound of 3 for comparisons. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several failure modes/limitations are discussed; reproducibility is reasonably supported by reported hyperparameters and dataset choice, though code availability is not mentioned.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments on Wikipedia and SCWS against appropriate baselines using standard metrics (Spearman, AvgSimC/MaxSimC), and the identified theoretical issue with a joint policy-gradient formulation is plausible and correctly noted. However, mathematical details are only summarized and completeness of derivations cannot be fully verified from the summary; the experimental methodology has weaknesses (no mention of statistical tests or multiple runs, and the non-parametric claim is undermined by imposing an upper bound of Q=3 in experiments). These gaps create reasonable doubts about some claims (especially automatic sense-number determination and robustness), so the contribution is not fully validated technically.  | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: training on a large Wikipedia corpus and evaluation on SCWS with appropriate baselines (including multi-sense and word2vec) and standard metrics (AvgSimC/MaxSimC, Spearman ρ). The theoretical critique of a joint policy-gradient formulation (logCo ≤ 0 can reduce probability of a correctly selected sense) is plausible and motivates their reward-passing Q-learning approach; no obvious mathematical errors are indicated in the summary. Minor concerns remain: the non-parametric claim is operationally constrained in experiments by an upper bound Q=3, exploration choices (dropout) and linear-time speed claims are asserted but not quantitatively demonstrated in the summary, and no mention of statistical significance testing or ablation studies is given. Overall the technical approach is sound with some experimental gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments on Wikipedia and SCWS against appropriate baselines using standard metrics (Spearman, AvgSimC/MaxSimC), and the identified theoretical issue with a joint policy-gradient formulation is plausible and correctly noted. However, mathematical details are only summarized and completeness of derivations cannot be fully verified from the summary; the experimental methodology has weaknesses (no mention of statistical tests or multiple runs, and the non-parametric claim is undermined by imposing an upper bound of Q=3 in experiments). These gaps create reasonable doubts about some claims (especially automatic sense-number determination and robustness), so the contribution is not fully validated technically.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: training on a large Wikipedia corpus and evaluation on SCWS with appropriate baselines (including multi-sense and word2vec) and standard metrics (AvgSimC/MaxSimC, Spearman ρ). The theoretical critique of a joint policy-gradient formulation (logCo ≤ 0 can reduce probability of a correctly selected sense) is plausible and motivates their reward-passing Q-learning approach; no obvious mathematical errors are indicated in the summary. Minor concerns remain: the non-parametric claim is operationally constrained in experiments by an upper bound Q=3, exploration choices (dropout) and linear-time speed claims are asserted but not quantitatively demonstrated in the summary, and no mention of statistical significance testing or ablation studies is given. Overall the technical approach is sound with some experimental gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of relevant baselines (Huang et al. 2012; Neelakantan et al. 2014; Tian et al. 2014; Li & Jurafsky 2015; Qiu et al. 2016; Bartunov et al. 2016; word2vec) and evaluates on standard data and metrics (Wikipedia corpus, SCWS, Spearman/AvgSimC/MaxSimC). The authors take steps to ensure fair comparisons (e.g., using an upper bound of 3 senses per word for parity with prior work), report comparable or state-of-the-art results, and explicitly discuss limitations and differences in training formulations, indicating thorough and fair analysis of competing approaches. | B(4): The paper compares against an appropriate and recent set of baselines (Huang et al. 2012; Neelakantan et al. 2014; Tian et al. 2014; Li & Jurafsky 2015; Qiu et al. 2016; Bartunov et al. 2016; word2vec) and evaluates on the same data and metrics (Wikipedia corpus, SCWS, Spearman/AvgSimC/MaxSimC). Comparisons appear fair (they constrain Q=3 for experiments to enable apples-to-apples comparison) and the manuscript analyzes methodological differences (Q-learning sense selection, linear-time selection, and drawbacks of joint policy-gradient). Related work coverage is thorough, though the summary does not clearly state whether the paper explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods match or outperform DRL-Sense, so I mark a small omission.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of relevant baselines (Huang et al. 2012; Neelakantan et al. 2014; Tian et al. 2014; Li & Jurafsky 2015; Qiu et al. 2016; Bartunov et al. 2016; word2vec) and evaluates on standard data and metrics (Wikipedia corpus, SCWS, Spearman/AvgSimC/MaxSimC). The authors take steps to ensure fair comparisons (e.g., using an upper bound of 3 senses per word for parity with prior work), report comparable or state-of-the-art results, and explicitly discuss limitations and differences in training formulations, indicating thorough and fair analysis of competing approaches.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against an appropriate and recent set of baselines (Huang et al. 2012; Neelakantan et al. 2014; Tian et al. 2014; Li & Jurafsky 2015; Qiu et al. 2016; Bartunov et al. 2016; word2vec) and evaluates on the same data and metrics (Wikipedia corpus, SCWS, Spearman/AvgSimC/MaxSimC). Comparisons appear fair (they constrain Q=3 for experiments to enable apples-to-apples comparison) and the manuscript analyzes methodological differences (Q-learning sense selection, linear-time selection, and drawbacks of joint policy-gradient). Related work coverage is thorough, though the summary does not clearly state whether the paper explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods match or outperform DRL-Sense, so I mark a small omission.

main_claims: Proposes DRL-Sense, a reinforcement-learning based framework composed of a sense selection module and a sense representation module for learning multi-sense word embeddings.

Enables efficient (linear-time) sense selection by exploiting word embeddings in the sense selection module and jointly trains sense selection and representation via a reward-passing Q-learning formulation.

Supports non-parametric learning that automatically determines the number of senses per word and produces competitive or state-of-the-art performance on contextual word similarity while matching word2vec performance using only 1/100 of training data (as claimed).
_summary.methodology: DRL-Sense uses two modules: a sense selection module formulated as an MDP and implemented with a Q-learning / Q-value neural model that scores senses from context embeddings, and a sense representation module trained with a skip-gram style collocation likelihood (negative sampling); senses are selected, used to update representations, and the resulting collocation likelihood is passed back as a reward to train the selection module jointly.
metrics: cross-entropy loss (training objective for selection module)
limitations: A joint policy-gradient formulation underestimates the probability of senses not yet selected during optimization (stated drawback of that formulation).

If optimized with stochastic gradient ascent on the joint log-likelihood, the update can decrease the probability of a correctly selected sense because the collocation log likelihood is non-positive (logCo ≤ 0).
Greedy sense selection may fail in early training due to exploration-exploitation trade-off (requires exploration such as dropout).
Experiments set an (upper bound) number of senses per word in Q to 3 for fair comparison, indicating an operational constraint in reported experiments.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - multi-sense word representations / word embeddings
